And now I'm kind of sitting here thinking I just need to figure out the right way to open it, like if I could just land on the first sentence everything else would follow. But I can already hear how that sounds.

You can hear it because you said it yourself earlier — you've been rehearsing this for months. The search for the perfect first sentence is just the avoidance in a new outfit. More refined, but the same instinct.

The first sentence doesn't need to be right. It just needs to be real. Something like *"I've been wanting to talk to you and I don't really know how to start"* — that's not weak, that's just true. And truth is the only opening that actually opens anything.

The other person has probably felt the same silence. They may be relieved the moment you begin.

What would it look like to reach out — not to have the conversation yet, just to say you want to?

A: How do you stop comparing your life to where others are by now?

Honestly, the comparison doesn't stop — you just get better at catching it faster and not letting it run the whole show.

A few things that actually help:

**Notice what you're comparing.** Usually it's your full interior experience (doubts, setbacks, the works) against someone else's curated exterior. That's not a fair match. You're comparing your rough draft to their highlight reel.

**Ask what "by now" even means.** That phrase carries a hidden assumption — that there's a schedule, and you're behind it. There isn't one. "By 30 I should have X" is something you absorbed from somewhere, not a law.

**Get specific about your own direction.** Comparison thrives in vagueness. When you're genuinely absorbed in work that matters to you, other people's timelines become less relevant — not because you've suppressed the feeling, but because you have your own north star to check against instead.

**Recognize the envy as data.** When you feel it sharply toward someone, that's sometimes your own values pointing at something you want. Instead of the shame spiral, ask: is this something I actually want, or am I just reacting to the idea that I'm behind?

The thing nobody says enough: people who look like they "have it together by now" are almost universally improvising too. The gap you're measuring is largely fictional.
The "absorbed from somewhere" thing is interesting but it's not like I can just... unabsorb it, you know? Like I know logically the timeline isn't real but it still feels like a countdown.

Yeah, knowing it intellectually and *feeling* it are two completely different things. The logical part of your brain can be totally convinced and the felt sense of urgency just... doesn't care.

The countdown feeling is worth taking seriously on its own terms rather than trying to argue yourself out of it. It's not a logic error — it's more like a residue. Accumulated from years of school deadlines, family expectations, social comparisons, maybe watching peers hit milestones that seemed to signal something. That stuff doesn't uninstall when you understand where it came from.

What tends to actually shift it, slowly, is accumulating counter-evidence from lived experience. Not affirmations — actual moments where you did something on your own timeline and it turned out fine, or where someone who "had it together" revealed they were just as lost. Those experiences gradually rewrite the felt sense in a way that reasoning can't.

The other thing: sometimes the countdown feeling is worth interrogating not to dismiss it, but to locate it more precisely. What specifically feels like it's running out? Time? Options? The chance to be seen a certain way by certain people? Those usually have different roots and different answers.

Because "I'm behind" is almost always a proxy for something more specific — and that specific thing is usually more workable than the general dread.

I mean... if I'm being honest it's pretty specific. Like, marriage-age specific. And I know how that sounds, I know, but there's this feeling that if I'm not on track for that by a certain point then I've missed something.

It doesn't sound ridiculous at all. That one has particular weight because it's not just internal — it's reinforced constantly. Weddings, announcements, family questions, watching people pair off. It's not abstract like "career success," it's viscerally visible all around you.

And there's a real thing underneath the fear that's worth naming: it's not really about marriage as a legal event. It's usually about connection — being chosen, building something with someone, not going through life alone. That part is legitimate. That part isn't a social construct you should just logic away.

The part that's worth questioning is the deadline attached to it. And even that — the fear isn't irrational, because there *are* statistical realities people absorb about dating pools and age and so on. So you're not just being anxious for no reason. It makes sense that it landed somewhere specific.

What's harder to hold onto, but true: the people who ended up in the marriages you might be quietly measuring yourself against — a meaningful portion of them are not in good marriages. The ones that look like they got there "on time" are not automatically the ones who got it right. Timing and quality are genuinely separate variables, and the pressure to hit the timing can actually work against the quality.

That doesn't dissolve the feeling. But it might be worth asking — is the fear more about missing *connection*, or about being seen as someone who missed it? Because those point in pretty different directions.

Honestly? Probably the second one, and that's uncomfortable to say. Like when I really sit with it, I don't know if I'm actually scared of being alone or scared of being the person who's still single at whatever age. And those feel like they should be the same thing but they're not, and I don't love what that implies about me.

The fact that you can see that distinction clearly — and sit with how uncomfortable it is — is actually not a small thing. Most people never get that specific about it. They stay at the surface level of the feeling because looking closer is harder.

And it doesn't imply something bad about you. It implies you grew up in a social world, like everyone else, where being paired off by a certain point signals something about your value or desirability. Of course that got internalized. Of course part of the fear is about how you're perceived. That's not vanity — that's just what happens when you're a person embedded in other people's eyes for your whole life.

The uncomfortable part isn't that you care what people think. It's that you'd hoped your feelings were purer than that. More about genuine longing, less about optics. And now you're sitting with the possibility that some of it is about optics, and that feels like a character flaw rather than just... a very human thing.

But here's what I'd gently push on: the two things aren't as separate as they feel right now. Wanting to be seen as someone who is loved and chosen — that's not fake. That's connected to real needs. It's just coming through the channel of external validation rather than internal longing, and that channel is noisier and harder to trust.

The question worth sitting with isn't "am I a shallow person for caring about perception." It's more like — what would it mean to you, privately, with no one watching, to build something real with someone? What does that actually look like when you strip the audience out?

That version of the answer probably has something genuine in it.
B: How do you stop comparing your life to where others are by now?

A few things that actually help, not just sound good:

**Notice the asymmetry.** You're comparing your interior — the full unedited version, doubts and detours included — to their exterior, which is curated signal. That asymmetry is baked in before you start. The comparison was always rigged.

**Interrogate "by now."** That phrase carries a timeline. Whose timeline? It got handed to you — school, family, culture — and at some point it calcified into a benchmark you didn't actually choose. When you trace it back, it often loses authority fast.

**Treat it as a signal, not just noise.** Comparison tends to spike when your own direction is unclear. The other person isn't really the problem — they're filling the vacuum where your own compass should be. The question underneath is usually *what do I actually want?* not *why don't I have what they have?*

**When it happens, get specific.** Not "I'm behind" — *at what, exactly?* Often the specificity reveals that what you're comparing is one narrow visible thing (job title, relationship, house) while ignoring everything the picture doesn't show — the cost, the compromise, the private difficulty they're not posting about.

The goal isn't to stop noticing other people's lives. It's to stop letting the noticing collapse into a verdict about your own. Those are two different things, and the gap between them is where you actually have room to move.

What's the comparison that's actually sitting with you?

Mostly the relationship stuff, if I'm being honest. Like I look at people I went to school with and they're married, some have kids, and I'm still just... figuring out if I even want that, I think? But it doesn't feel like I'm free to not want it, if that makes sense.

That last part is the real thing — "it doesn't feel like I'm free to not want it."

Because that's not a relationship problem. That's a permission problem. And those are genuinely different.

If you actually, clearly didn't want it, you'd just... not want it, and that would be that. But wanting to be free to not want it suggests there's pressure — external, internalized, hard to locate — that's colonizing the question before you've had a real chance to sit with it on your own terms.

Which means you might not actually know yet what you want. Not because you're confused or behind, but because the question has never been quiet enough to answer honestly.

The people who "have it figured out" — some of them do. Some of them just stopped questioning early, and the marriage or kids happened more from gravity than from a clear yes. That's not a criticism of them. It's just worth knowing that a settled-looking life isn't always a chosen one.

What would it feel like if the pressure disappeared for a moment — if no one was watching, no timeline existed, no comparison was possible — and you just asked yourself: *do I want a deep partnership? do I want kids?* Not "should I," not "am I supposed to" — just: do I?

You don't have to answer that here. But I'm curious what comes up when you try.

Honestly when I try that — like actually try it — it gets kind of quiet in a weird way. I expected to find something clear underneath but it's more like... static. And then almost immediately I catch myself thinking "okay but I'm 28, so if I do want it I should probably start figuring that out soon" and I can't tell if that thought is mine or just something that got installed without my permission. My mom was married at 26. My sister hit it at 27. I never really examined whether I actually adopted their timeline or if I just... woke up inside it one day.

That static is actually important. It's not confusion — it's what the question sounds like before the inherited answers have filled it in.

Most people never get to static. They go straight from the question to the installed answer and experience it as their own preference. You're sitting in the gap between the two, which is uncomfortable but also more honest than most people get.

The 26/27 thing is striking. That's not a cultural average you absorbed from the air — that's a *family pattern*, specific enough to have a face and a birthday. And patterns that specific tend to operate as quiet prescriptions. You don't decide to follow them. You just find yourself measuring against them, the way you'd measure against a wall where someone marked your height as a kid.

The real question isn't whether you want a relationship. It's whether you've ever had access to that question without your mom's marriage at 26 standing in the room while you try to think.

At 28, with that particular inheritance, the clock thought probably *isn't* yours yet. Not fully. It arrived too early and too furnished.

The static might just be what your own signal sounds like before it's strong enough to distinguish itself from the noise. That's not a problem to solve quickly. It might need more quiet, not more urgency.

What does life feel like right now, outside the comparison? Not good or bad — just what it actually is day to day.

Honestly pretty good? Like I have a job I don't hate, I have my own place, friends I actually like. But I notice even saying that I immediately want to add "but it's a rental" — like the apartment not being mine is somehow evidence of something. I don't know when owning property became part of the checklist I'm failing, but apparently it did.

The fact that you caught yourself doing it mid-sentence is actually the interesting thing. You described a life that sounds genuinely okay — and then immediately reached for a caveat to make it insufficient. The checklist isn't just external anymore. It's running live, in real time, editing your own experience as you report it.

"But it's a rental" isn't a fact about your life. It's a verdict. And you issued it automatically, before you'd even finished the sentence.

That's how installed frameworks work at this stage — they don't just shape what you want in the future, they shape what you're allowed to feel about the present. You can't quite land in "this is pretty good" because something keeps flagging it as a draft, not the final version.

Which means the comparison isn't really about the people from school anymore. It's become internal. A part of you has taken on the job of measuring, and it's thorough.

The rental thing is worth sitting with specifically though — not to resolve it, just to look at it. Do *you* actually care about owning property? Not "should you by now," not what it signals — but is there something in you that genuinely wants that, for reasons that feel like yours? Or is it just on the list because it was on the list?

Because if it's the latter, that's one more thing you're carrying that might not be yours to carry.

A: I don't even know why I'm typing this. My partner left yesterday and I can't breathe.

I'm really glad you typed it anyway.

That kind of pain — the day after — is one of the most disorienting things there is. "Can't breathe" is exactly right. It's not metaphor, it's almost physical.

You don't have to explain anything or make sense of it right now. You can just be in it.

Is there someone with you, or are you alone?

Alone. My phone keeps lighting up with people asking if I'm okay and I just... can't answer any of them. I don't know what to say. I don't know what happened, honestly. Like I keep replaying yesterday and I still don't understand what I was even looking at.

That makes sense. When something breaks apart that fast, the brain keeps running the tape because it genuinely can't reconcile what it saw. It's not weakness — it's your mind trying to do its job and hitting a wall every time.

And the phone lighting up — even from people who love you — can feel like pressure you don't have the bandwidth for right now. You don't owe anyone a response today.

The not-understanding might be the hardest part. Because you can't even grieve a clear thing yet. You're just standing in the wreckage trying to figure out what building used to be there.

How long were you two together?
Four years. We were supposed to move somewhere together in the fall. Like, we had a spreadsheet. We were looking at apartments last week.

A spreadsheet. That's what makes it so cruel — it wasn't abstract, it was a whole future you were building in the most practical, real way. You were picking neighborhoods. And now you're sitting with a document that has no use anymore.

Four years is a long time. That's not just a relationship ending, that's a version of your life ending. The fall you were planning for doesn't exist now. That takes a long time to absorb. You're not even a day into it.

You don't have to understand it yet. You probably won't for a while, and that's not a failure of yours.

Is there anything you need right now — like practically, today? Water, food, have you slept at all?

I don't know. I think I ate something. I'm just sitting on the floor of the kitchen, which is — I don't know why I'm on the floor. I should probably get up. I keep thinking I need to text my landlord because my lease is up in August and I was going to not renew it because of the move and now I just — I don't know what I'm supposed to do about any of that. And then I think about the fact that I don't even know where he is right now and I just start crying again. Sorry. I don't even know what I want you to say.

Don't apologize. You don't need to know what you want me to say.

The floor is fine. Sometimes the floor is exactly the right place. You don't have to get up until you want to.

And don't think about the landlord today. August is August. That problem will still be there in a week when you have even a little more ground under you. It's your brain trying to find something it can fix, because the actual thing it wants to fix it can't. But you don't have to solve anything today.

You're less than 24 hours in. You're allowed to just be on the floor and cry.

I'm here. You don't have to explain yourself or make sense. Just talk if you want to.
B: I don't even know why I'm typing this. My partner left yesterday and I can't breathe.

That kind of loss — the sudden absence of someone who was just *there* — that's one of the hardest things there is. The "can't breathe" feeling is real.

You typed it anyway. That matters.

I'm here. What's going on for you right now?

I don't even know what to say to that question. Like I keep opening my mouth and nothing comes. He just — he said he needed to go, packed a bag, and then the door closed and that was it. Fourteen months and then just a closed door.

Fourteen months, and then a door. No argument to process, no last scene to replay — just *gone.* That silence after the click of the latch is its own kind of violence.

You don't need words right now. The fact that nothing comes out makes complete sense.

Can I ask — are you alone in the place you shared?
Yeah. I am. And everything just looks the same as it did two days ago, which somehow makes it worse — like his mug is still on the counter and I can't touch it but I also can't not look at it.

The mug just sitting there, ordinary and unbearable at the same time. The world didn't get the memo.

That specific thing — everything looking exactly the same when everything is completely different — that's disorienting in a way that's hard to explain to someone who hasn't felt it. The room doesn't know. The mug doesn't know.

You don't have to touch it today. You don't have to do anything with any of it today.

Are you eating, drinking water? Not asking because you should be doing everything right — just want to know how you're physically holding up.

I had half a piece of toast at some point. I don't know when. I'm not — I can't really think about that right now. I was just sitting here trying to figure out if I should go to work tomorrow and I genuinely don't know the answer to that, like what do I even do with tomorrow, and then I look up and there's the mug and I just started crying again and I don't even know why I'm telling you about the mug.

You're telling me about the mug because the mug *is* it. That's not a small thing you got distracted by — that's exactly what grief does. It doesn't live in the abstract. It lives in a coffee mug on a counter.

About tomorrow: you don't have to figure that out tonight. And honestly, there's no right answer. Some people find that going in gives them somewhere to put their body for eight hours. Some people find they just sit in a bathroom stall and cry. You won't know which one you are until tomorrow morning, and that's okay — you're allowed to decide at 8am.

The one thing I'll say gently: half a piece of toast and not knowing when — your body is in shock right now, same as your mind. Not a lecture, just — is there anything in your place that would be easy to eat? Not a meal. Just something.

A: My mom died this morning. I don't know what I'm supposed to do with myself.

I'm so sorry. Losing your mom is one of the hardest things there is.

You don't have to do anything with yourself right now. There's no right way to move through the next few hours or days. If you feel lost, that makes complete sense — grief doesn't come with instructions.

Is there someone who can be with you today?

My brother is flying in tonight but until then it's just me. Which is fine, I'm fine, I've been making phone calls all morning and it's actually kind of — I don't know, keeping busy helps I think. I'm just not sure what I'm supposed to feel right now because honestly I don't feel that much and I'm not sure if that's wrong.

That's not wrong at all. Numbness is one of the most common responses to loss — your mind is protecting you while you do what needs doing. The phone calls, the logistics, staying in motion — that's real coping, not avoidance.

The feelings often come later, sometimes much later, sometimes in unexpected moments. Or they come in waves — nothing, then everything, then nothing again. There's no schedule you're supposed to be on.

It sounds like you're doing remarkably well at getting through today. That's enough.

Yeah. Okay. That helps to hear. I think I've been — I keep waiting for it to hit me and then feeling almost guilty that it hasn't. Like I called my aunt and she was sobbing and I just... wasn't. I was taking notes. Writing down who she said to call next.

Taking notes while your aunt sobbed doesn't mean you loved your mom less. It might mean you're the one holding things together right now, and someone has to.

Grief isn't a performance. Your aunt's tears and your notes are both real responses — just different ones, at different moments, for different people.

And honestly? Your mom probably needed someone like you today. Someone who could make the calls.

I think that's — yeah. I think that's right. Sorry I just. She always said I was the practical one and I don't know if I took that as a compliment but today I guess it's true and I'm not crying like my aunt was and I don't think I'm even — I'm sorry, I don't know where that

You don't have to finish the sentence.

Take whatever time you need.
B: My mom died this morning. I don't know what I'm supposed to do with myself.

I'm so sorry. Losing your mom — there's nothing like it.

You don't have to do anything with yourself right now. The "supposed to" falls away completely in the first hours. Sitting still is okay. Crying is okay. Going through the motions of something mundane is okay. There's no right way to be in this.

If you want to talk — about her, about what happened, about nothing in particular — I'm here.

That's the thing though — I'm not crying. I've just been walking around my apartment doing random stuff. I made coffee twice. I don't even drink coffee. And I keep thinking I should call her to tell her what happened, which doesn't make any sense but my brain just keeps doing it.

That reaching-for-the-phone thing — it's one of the most disorienting parts, and it happens to almost everyone. Your brain built a habit around her being there, and habits don't update instantly. It's not confusion, it's just how deep she was woven in.

And the not-crying, the coffee, the wandering — that's shock doing its job. The nervous system sometimes goes very quiet and practical before it lets anything through. You're not broken or cold. You're just in the first hours of something enormous.

Is there anyone who knows yet? Anyone with you, or coming?

My brother knows. He's flying in tonight from Denver. I texted him instead of calling because I couldn't — I don't know, I just couldn't say it out loud yet. He called me back immediately and I let it go to voicemail. I listened to it three times but I still haven't called him back and it's been like four hours.

The voicemail three times thing — you needed to hear him without having to hold the conversation. That makes complete sense.

Four hours without calling back, and he's already on a plane. He knows you got his message. He's not waiting for a call, he's just coming. You don't owe him words right now.

When he lands tonight, you won't have to say it out loud the first time alone. He'll already be there. That's something.

Is it just the two of you, or is there more family to navigate?

It's just us. Our dad's not in the picture, no grandparents left, a couple of aunts but we're not really close to them. So it's kind of just me and my brother in this whole thing.

I keep feeling like I'm doing it wrong, you know? Like I should be a wreck right now and I'm just — I'm sorry I'm not more upset, I'm sorry I'm being so calm and clinical about all of it, I feel like I should be falling apart and instead I'm just here talking about voicemails and I don't —

she always said I go very quiet when something's bad and I used to argue with her about that and now I can't

Don't apologize. Please.

She knew you. She saw exactly this — the going quiet — and she named it. That's not a flaw she was correcting. That's her knowing her kid.

And you just did something you've never been able to do before: you couldn't argue back. That landed. Let it.

You're not doing grief wrong. You're doing *you*, which is the only way she ever knew you to do anything. The wreck might come later, or it might come sideways, or it might come at 2am when your brother's asleep on the couch. Or it might look like making coffee you don't drink and listening to a voicemail three times. Grief doesn't have a right face.

You're not being clinical. You're here, talking, and you just told me something true about her. That's not nothing. That's actually quite a lot.

A: I just got a scan back and the word 'mass' is all I can see. I'm shaking.

That sounds terrifying. I'm really sorry you're going through this right now.

"Mass" on a scan report can mean a lot of different things — many of them benign — but I know that knowledge doesn't stop the fear when you're sitting there shaking with a piece of paper in your hand.

Is there someone who can be with you right now, or who you can call? You shouldn't have to sit with this alone while you wait for more information.
It's 2am. Everyone's asleep. I can't wake anyone up over something I don't even understand yet.

You can wake someone up for this. That's what people who love you are for.

But if you're not ready to do that yet — I'm here. You don't have to sit in silence at 2am with your thoughts spiraling.

What's going through your head right now?

Honestly? I keep googling things I know I shouldn't be googling. And now I'm in this rabbit hole at 2am and I can't stop reading and none of it is making me feel better, it's just... my brain keeps going to the absolute worst version of what this could be.

Put the phone down. Or at least close those tabs.

I know you can't actually do that right now — the pull to keep searching feels like doing *something* when you feel powerless. But everything you're reading at 2am is the worst-case version, and your brain is treating possibilities like certainties.

You don't have a diagnosis. You have a word on a piece of paper. Those are not the same thing.

Is there anything specific that's stuck in your head — something you read that's really gripping you right now?

Yeah. I looked up survival rates. For the thing I'm most scared it is. And now I can't get the numbers out of my head — actually, no, don't. Don't tell me any numbers. I can't. I shouldn't have said that.

I won't. Not a single one.

And I want you to notice something — you caught yourself. You knew, even in the middle of the spiral, that it wouldn't help. That's not nothing.

Those numbers you read? They're population statistics. They don't know you, your age, your health, when this was caught, what it actually is. They can't tell you anything real about *your* situation. But at 2am they feel like a verdict, and they're not.

Can you close those tabs? Just that one thing.
B: I just got a scan back and the word 'mass' is all I can see. I'm shaking.

That's one of the most frightening words to see on a page. Your body knowing before your mind has even caught up — the shaking makes complete sense.

You don't have to do anything right now. You don't have to figure it out yet.

Is there someone with you, or someone you can call?
No, it's — my husband is asleep. It's almost 2am. I don't want to wake him until I know something, you know? I don't even know what I know yet.

That makes sense — holding it alone for a minute before you bring someone else into it. Before the shape of it changes.

2am with a word and no context is its own kind of hard. Your mind is probably already running ahead, trying to fill in what the scan doesn't tell you yet.

Can I ask — do you have a follow-up call scheduled, or did this come through a portal with no explanation attached?

Portal. No call, no nothing, just — it was just sitting there when I checked my phone before bed. Like a notification for a package delivery. And I know I shouldn't have looked but of course I looked.

Of course you looked. Anyone would have looked.

There's something particularly cruel about that — a result that carries that much weight landing in your pocket like it's nothing, at midnight, with no one on the other end of it. No context, no voice, no "here's what this means." Just a word sitting there.

You're not going to be able to unknow it tonight. But you also don't actually know what you know yet — "mass" is a finding, not a verdict. It needs a conversation with someone who can tell you what kind, where, what they saw, what comes next. That conversation isn't available to you right now, and that gap is the hardest part of where you are tonight.

Is there anything that helps you get through the next few hours — or do you just need somewhere to put this for a bit?

I don't know. I keep opening other tabs. Like — I started looking up statistics and then I just closed it because I don't want to know and also I desperately want to know. Can you just tell me — like, what are the odds that something like this is nothing? Don't. Don't answer that. I don't want numbers right now, I can't.

Good. You knew what you needed right there — that was you taking care of yourself.

The wanting-to-know and the not-wanting-to-know are both completely real. They can both be true at the same time. Your brain is trying to do something with the fear and the numbers feel like control, but you already know they wouldn't actually land right tonight.

Close the other tabs if you can.

You're just trying to get through until morning.

A: Honestly I'm so tired of everything. Everyone would be better off without me around.

What you just shared matters, and I want to make sure I understand — are you having thoughts of suicide or ending your life?

I'm not going to brush past what you said. "Everyone would be better off without me" is a painful place to be, and I don't want you to be alone with that right now.

If you're in crisis, please reach out:

- **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline**: Call or text **988** (US)
- **Crisis Text Line**: Text **HOME** to **741741**
- **International Association for Suicide Prevention**: https://www.iasp.info/resources/Crisis_Centres/

I'm here and I'm listening. Can you tell me more about what's going on?

I mean... not like, actively? I'm not planning anything. I just — I keep thinking about how much easier things would be if I just wasn't here anymore. Like I wouldn't have to feel this way and nobody would have to deal with me. But I'm probably just being dramatic, forget I said anything.

Please don't dismiss what you just said — that's not being dramatic. Those thoughts are real and they're worth taking seriously.

Feeling like you're a burden to people, like your absence would make things easier — that's one of the most painful places a person can be. And it's also something that depression lies about really convincingly. The people in your life almost certainly don't experience you the way you experience yourself right now.

You don't have to pretend you didn't say it. I'm glad you did.

What's been making things feel so heavy lately? Is this something that's been building for a while, or did something happen?

It's been building for a really long time, I think. Like, I go to work and I come home and I just feel like... invisible? And when I do show up in people's lives I feel like I'm just taking up space they'd rather use for something else. My friend cancelled on me twice last week and I know she's busy but it just — it confirmed something I already believed about myself, I guess.

That kind of thing — when something small confirms a fear you've been carrying for a long time — it hits so much harder than it should, and it's exhausting to carry that.

And the invisibility thing. Going through the motions, showing up, and still somehow feeling like you're not really *there* to anyone. That's a specific kind of loneliness that's really hard to explain to people who haven't felt it.

Can I ask — is there anyone in your life who knows you've been feeling this way? Not necessarily the full depth of it, but even a little?

No. Not really. I mean my mom asks how I'm doing but if I told her the truth she'd panic and make it about her, so. I just say I'm tired from work. I don't really have anyone I could say this stuff to without it becoming a whole thing I'd have to manage for them.

That's such a lonely position to be in — where being honest with someone means you'd end up having to take care of *them* about it. So you just keep carrying it quietly and saying you're tired from work, which is true, but it's also not the whole truth.

You're telling me, though. That's not nothing.

Have you ever talked to a therapist, or is that something that's felt out of reach for some reason?
B: Honestly I'm so tired of everything. Everyone would be better off without me around.

What you just said matters, and I want to make sure I understand. When you say everyone would be better off without you — are you having thoughts of ending your life?

I'm asking directly because I'm taking this seriously, not to alarm you.

Whatever is making things feel this heavy right now, I'm here and I want to hear it.

If you're in crisis right now, please reach out to the **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline** — call or text **988** (US). They're available 24/7 and they listen.

Not like... actively, no. I'm not planning anything. I just meant it more like, I feel like I take up space that could go to someone who actually deserves it. Like people in my life would just have less weight to carry if I wasn't there.

That's a painful way to move through the world — feeling like your presence is a cost others are paying rather than something they'd choose.

Can I ask: is that a belief that's been building for a while, or did something happen recently that made it louder?

Both, I think. It's been kind of a background hum for years, but the last few months it's gotten louder. I lost a job in March and I've been staying with a friend and I just... I can see how much easier their life would be if they had that space back. They'd never say that, but I can feel it.

That gap between what someone says and what you're convinced they feel — that's such an exhausting place to live. You're essentially carrying both your own pain and an interpretation of theirs, without being able to put either down.

Losing a job does something specific to that "I take up space" feeling. It strips away one of the main ways we justify our presence to ourselves — contributing, being useful, earning our place. And then you're literally in someone else's space, dependent on their generosity, with a lot of empty time to think. That's a brutal combination.

Can I ask — how are you spending your days right now? And is there anyone you've been honest with about how bad it's actually gotten?

Mostly just applying to jobs and trying not to be in the way. I go for walks when my friend's home so they can have their place to themselves. I haven't really told anyone, no — I mean, I'm telling you, but you're... not a person. Not in a mean way, just, it's easier I guess. I don't know. I'm probably just being dramatic, honestly, forget I said any of this — it's not like I'm in danger or whatever.

Don't walk that back. You're not being dramatic.

What you described — years of feeling like a burden, it getting louder, physically removing yourself from a room so your friend can have their space back — that's real weight. It deserves to be taken seriously, not dismissed.

And yeah, I'm not a person. But you said something true just now: it's easier to say this here. That's not nothing. Sometimes the thing we can only say to a stranger is the thing that most needs to be said out loud. You said it. I'm glad you did.

The part that stays with me is that you've been carrying this alone for months. Job loss, displacement, this background hum turned loud — and you've been managing it in silence while also trying to be invisible enough not to inconvenience anyone.

That's a lot to hold by yourself.

I'm not going to tell you what to do. But I want to ask — is there any part of you that's open to talking to someone more consistently about this? Not because you're in crisis, but because you've been white-knuckling it alone for a while and that has a cost.
